era_context: Early Bronze Age III in the Levant and Egypt; the Old Kingdom of Egypt under the late Fourth Dynasty (c. 2613–2494 BCE). Pyramid construction at Giza was at or past its peak. In Mesopotamia, the Early Dynastic III period was underway, with city-states competing for regional dominance. The Indus Valley Civilization was in its mature phase. Writing systems (Egyptian hieroglyphics, cuneiform, proto-Indus script) were well established.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited archaeological record for this period)
Northern Europe (pre-literate societies, sparse archaeological coverage)
East Asia beyond the Indus Valley (limited contemporary records)
The Americas (no written records; archaeological evidence sparse and difficult to date precisely)

events.title: Reign of Khafre, Fourth Dynasty Egypt
events.description: Khafre (also Chephren) ruled Egypt during the Fourth Dynasty, a period of continued monumental construction and centralized pharaonic power. He commissioned the Great Sphinx of Giza and the Pyramid of Khafre, consolidating the architectural legacy of his predecessors. His reign exemplified the height of Old Kingdom administrative capacity and resource mobilization. The exact dates of his reign are debated, but he is traditionally placed c. 2558–2532 BCE; this year falls within the probable range of his rule.

sources.name: Abydos King List (Temple of Seti I)
Archaeological evidence from Giza (pyramid, sphinx, temple complexes)

events.certainty_note: Khafre's reign is well attested in later king lists and monumental evidence, but absolute dating relies on astronomical calculations and radiocarbon dating with ±50–100 year margins. Placement in 2679 BCE is conventional but uncertain by ±30–50 years.

events.title: Mature Indus Valley Civilization urban centers
events.region: Indus Valley (modern Pakistan and northwest India)
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization was at its mature phase, with major urban centers such as Harappa and Mohenjo-daro functioning as planned cities with standardized weights, measures, and script. Trade networks extended to Mesopotamia and the Persian Gulf. This period saw sophisticated urban planning, drainage systems, and craft production. The civilization's undeciphered script remains one of archaeology's major unsolved problems.

sources.name: Archaeological excavations at Harappa, Mohenjo-daro, and other Indus sites

events.certainty_note: The mature phase of the Indus Valley Civilization is archaeologically well-attested through stratigraphy and radiocarbon dating. Absolute dating places the mature phase c. 2600–1900 BCE; 2679 BCE falls within this range with moderate confidence (±50–100 years).

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Khafre's reign is contested. Some Egyptologists place him c. 2558–2532 BCE; others argue for c. 2520–2494 BCE. Radiocarbon dating of organic materials from the pyramids has produced conflicting results, with some studies suggesting dates up to 100 years earlier than traditional Egyptological chronologies. The assignment of this year to Khafre's reign is therefore approximate and subject to ongoing revision.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 2679 BCE is heavily dependent on monumental archaeology and later king lists compiled centuries or millennia after the events. Egyptian chronology for the Old Kingdom remains one of Egyptology's most contentious issues, with competing 'high,' 'middle,' and 'low' chronologies differing by up to 200 years. The Indus Valley Civilization lacks written historical records (its script is undeciphered), so understanding of this period relies entirely on archaeological stratigraphy and material culture. Mesopotamian records for this year are sparse and difficult to correlate with Egyptian chronology.

graph_edges.note: Khafre's reign in Egypt was contemporary with the mature phase of the Indus Valley Civilization, though no direct contact is documented.
